items.q: What do the arrows in a food chain represent?
items.correct: The arrows show the direction of energy transfer, pointing from the organism eaten to the one that eats it.
items.misconception: The arrows point toward the animal that gets eaten.
items.random_wrong: The arrows show which animals live together.
items.why: Students reverse the arrow direction, not realising it follows energy flow.

items.q: Why are there usually fewer top predators than producers?
items.correct: Energy is lost at each trophic level, so less energy is available to support fewer organisms higher up the chain.
items.misconception: There are fewer predators simply because they are bigger animals.
items.random_wrong: There are fewer predators only because they reproduce slowly.
items.why: Students explain the pyramid shape by body size rather than energy loss.

items.q: What is the role of decomposers?
items.correct: Decomposers break down dead organisms and waste, releasing nutrients back into the soil for plants to reuse.
items.misconception: Decomposers are the top predators in the food chain.
items.random_wrong: Decomposers make their own food by photosynthesis.
items.why: Students don't recognise decomposers as nutrient recyclers.

items.q: In a food chain, the arrows show the direction of:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: energy transfer. “predator movement” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Most energy is lost between trophic levels mainly through:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: respiration and heat loss. “photosynthesis” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: estimate the number of organisms in an area by sampling
items.misconception: measure the temperature of a habitat
items.why: Correct answer: estimate the number of organisms in an area by sampling. “measure the temperature of a habitat” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Decomposers are important in an ecosystem because they:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: recycle nutrients from dead material. “produce food by photosynthesis” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A producer level contains 10000 kJ of energy and 10% passes to the primary consumers. How much energy do they receive?
items.why: The real figure is 1000 kJ. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
